content.text:
Web search isn't configured. I'll provide a comprehensive analysis based on technical specifications and established engineering knowledge:

---

## Real-Time Communication Techniques: Comparison

### 1. WebSockets

**How It Works:**
- Starts as HTTP handshake, then upgrades to persistent TCP connection
- Full-duplex: both client and server can send messages independently
- Binary and text frames over a single socket
- Maintains connection until explicitly closed

**Pros:**
- ✅ Lowest latency once connected
- ✅ Full-duplex communication
- ✅ Minimal overhead per message (2-14 byte frame header)
- ✅ True push from server to client

**Cons:**
- ❌ Complex protocol (handshake, framing, masking)
- ❌ Requires dedicated connection (separate from HTTP)
- ❌ Harder to proxy/load balance (needs WebSocket-aware infrastructure)
- ❌ More challenging to secure (CSRF, origin validation needed)

**When to Use:**
- Real-time games, chat, collaborative editing
- High-frequency bidirectional data (trading platforms)
- When you need minimal latency

**Browser Support:** Excellent—supported by all modern browsers since ~2012

**Scalability:**
- Connection per client = memory/thread overhead
- Typical limit: ~10K-100K concurrent connections per server (depending on implementation)
- Requires WebSocket-aware load balancers (HAProxy, nginx with upgrade config)

---

### 2. Server-Sent Events (SSE)

**How It Works:**
- HTTP-based one-way stream from server to client
- Uses `text/event-stream` content type
- Client connects once, server keeps connection open and sends events
- Auto-reconnect with `Last-Event-ID` for missed events

**Pros:**
- ✅ Simple protocol (just HTTP + specific format)
- ✅ Works through standard HTTP proxies
- ✅ Built-in reconnection and event IDs
- ✅ Native browser API (`EventSource`)
- ✅ Automatic HTTP/2 multiplexing benefits

**Cons:**
- ❌ Server-to-client only (unidirectional)
- ❌ 6 concurrent connection limit per domain (HTTP/1.1)
- ❌ No binary support (text only)
- ❌ IE not supported, limited mobile browser support

**When to Use:**
- News feeds, live updates, dashboards
- Server notifications, progress bars
- When you only need server→client push

**Browser Support:** Good—Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge. No IE. Mobile Safari has quirks.

**Scalability:**
- HTTP/2 dramatically improves this (multiplexing over single connection)
- Standard HTTP load balancing works
- Can handle higher connection counts than WebSockets in some architectures

---

### 3. Long Polling

**How It Works:**
- Client makes HTTP request, server holds it open until data available
- Server responds, then client immediately makes new request
- Emulates push via repeated request/response cycles

**Pros:**
- ✅ Works everywhere (old browsers, restrictive proxies)
- ✅ Standard HTTP—no special infrastructure needed
- ✅ Firewall/proxy friendly
- ✅ Simple to implement

**Cons:**
- ❌ Higher latency (must wait for next poll cycle)
- ❌ More overhead (HTTP headers on every request)
- ❌ Server holds resources waiting for each poll
- ❌ Timing trade-offs (fast polling = overhead, slow = latency)

**When to Use:**
- Legacy browser support required
- Restricted corporate networks
- Occasional updates (not real-time)
- Fallback when WebSockets/SSE unavailable

**Browser Support:** Universal

**Scalability:**
- Poor for high-frequency updates
- Creates many short-lived connections
- HTTP/1.1 connection limits apply per poll
- Harder on server resources than persistent connections

---

## Quick Comparison Table

| Aspect | WebSockets | SSE | Long Polling |
|--------|-----------|-----|--------------|
| Direction | Bidirectional | Server→Client | Emulated bidirectional |
| Latency | Lowest | Low | Medium-High |
| Protocol | WS/WSS | HTTP(S) | HTTP(S) |
| Connection | Persistent | Persistent | Repeated |
| Overhead | Minimal | Low | Higher |
| Proxy Friendly | Moderate | Yes | Yes |
| Binary Data | Yes | No | Base64-able |
| Auto-reconnect | Manual | Built-in | Manual |
| Mobile/Battery | Good drain | Better | Worst |

---

## Decision Framework

1. **Need true bidirectional with minimal latency?** → WebSockets
2. **Just need server pushes to browser?** → SSE (use HTTP/2)
3. **Legacy support or restrictive networks?** → Long Polling
4. **High-scale server→client only?** → SSE with HTTP/2
5. **Games/real-time collaboration?** → WebSockets

---

Sources: RFC 6455 (WebSockets), WHATWG HTML Standard (SSE), HTTP/2 RFC 7540, MDN Web Docs for browser compatibility data.

I've already completed this task. Here's my research:

---

## Real-Time Communication Techniques: Comparison

### 1. WebSockets
**How It Works:**
- Starts as HTTP handshake, then upgrades to persistent TCP connection
- Full-duplex: both client and server can send messages independently
- Binary and text frames over a single socket
- Maintains connection until explicitly closed

**Pros:**
- ✅ Lowest latency once connected
- ✅ Full-duplex communication
- ✅ Minimal overhead per message (2-14 byte frame header)
- ✅ True push from server to client

**Cons:**
- ❌ Complex protocol (handshake, framing, masking)
- ❌ Requires dedicated connection (separate from HTTP)
- ❌ Harder to proxy/load balance (needs WebSocket-aware infrastructure)
- ❌ More challenging to secure (CSRF, origin validation needed)

**When to Use:** Real-time games, chat, collaborative editing, high-frequency bidirectional data (trading platforms)

**Browser Support:** Excellent—all modern browsers since ~2012

**Scalability:** Connection per client = memory/thread overhead. Typical limit: ~10K-100K concurrent connections per server

---

### 2. Server-Sent Events (SSE)
**How It Works:**
- HTTP-based one-way stream from server to client
- Uses `text/event-stream` content type
- Client connects once, server keeps connection open and sends events
- Auto-reconnect with `Last-Event-ID` for missed events

**Pros:**
- ✅ Simple protocol (just HTTP + specific format)
- ✅ Works through standard HTTP proxies
- ✅ Built-in reconnection and event IDs
- ✅ Native browser API (`EventSource`)
- ✅ Automatic HTTP/2 multiplexing benefits

**Cons:**
- ❌ Server-to-client only (unidirectional)
- ❌ 6 concurrent connection limit per domain (HTTP/1.1)
- ❌ No binary support (text only)
- ❌ IE not supported, limited mobile browser support

**When to Use:** News feeds, live updates, dashboards, server notifications, progress bars

**Browser Support:** Good—Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge. No IE. Mobile Safari has quirks.

**Scalability:** HTTP/2 dramatically improves this. Standard HTTP load balancing works.

---

### 3. Long Polling
**How It Works:**
- Client makes HTTP request, server holds it open until data available
- Server responds, then client immediately makes new request
- Emulates push via repeated request/response cycles

**Pros:**
- ✅ Works everywhere (old browsers, restrictive proxies)
- ✅ Standard HTTP—no special infrastructure needed
- ✅ Firewall/proxy friendly
- ✅ Simple to implement

**Cons:**
- ❌ Higher latency (must wait for next poll cycle)
- ❌ More overhead (HTTP headers on every request)
- ❌ Server holds resources waiting for each poll
- ❌ Timing trade-offs (fast polling = overhead, slow = latency)

**When to Use:** Legacy browser support required, restricted corporate networks, occasional updates

**Browser Support:** Universal

**Scalability:** Poor for high-frequency updates. Creates many short-lived connections.

---

## Decision Framework
1. **Need true bidirectional with minimal latency?** → WebSockets
2. **Just need server pushes to browser?** → SSE (use HTTP/2)
3. **Legacy support or restrictive networks?** → Long Polling
4. **High-scale server→client only?** → SSE with HTTP/2
5. **Games/real-time collaboration?** → WebSockets
